key_insight: Personalizing a soft robotic exoglove to a user’s hand geometry improves fit and helps target finger joint motion for dexterous rehabilitation.
engineering_relevance: The work shows how anatomically informed design, FEA, and pressure control can be combined to optimize soft wearable actuators for hand therapy and assistance.
method: Topological hand scans were used to tailor the exoglove geometry and fabricate silicone molds.
Finite element analysis modeled actuator bending and physical human-robot interaction against a personalized finger model.
Pneumatic experiments tested static and dynamic pressure-based flexion of MCP and PIP joints.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that scans enabled precise anatomical tailoring, simulations captured contact forces and joint mobilization, and tests showed accurate targeted movement of MCP and PIP joints.
watch_item: Performance may still depend on managing non-ideal compression and actuator-to-finger misalignment, especially across different hand anatomies and stiffness levels.
paper_title: Soft Robotic Exogloves for Dexterous Mobility -- Towards Personalized Rehabilitation
